locked_facts: Component along plane: mg sinθ (drives motion down the incline)
Component perpendicular to plane: mg cosθ (balanced by normal force N)
Normal force N = mg cosθ (NOT mg on an incline)
With friction (sliding down): a = g(sinθ - μ cosθ)
Angle of repose: θ_r = tan⁻¹(μ_s) — block just begins to slide
For θ < θ_r: block stays at rest. For θ > θ_r: block accelerates down
truth_statements: On an inclined plane the weight must be resolved into components parallel and perpendicular to the surface
The normal force on an incline is mg cosθ which is less than mg — this is why friction is reduced on steeper inclines
Without friction the acceleration down an incline is g sinθ independent of the mass of the object
The angle of repose is the angle at which the component of gravity along the plane just equals maximum static friction
Friction on an incline acts up the plane when the object tends to slide down and can act down the plane if the object is pushed up

aha_moment.what_to_show: Free body diagram on an incline with weight mg resolved into mg sinθ (along plane) and mg cosθ (perpendicular). As θ increases: mg sinθ grows, mg cosθ shrinks, N decreases, friction capacity (μN = μmg cosθ) decreases. At angle of repose: mg sinθ = μmg cosθ → tanθ = μ.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Animated FBD with changing θ — the parallel component grows while friction capacity shrinks until they cross at angle of repose
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students struggle with force resolution — seeing the components change continuously with angle makes the physics intuitive

simulation_states.label: Force resolution
simulation_states.description: Block on incline. Weight mg resolved into mg sinθ (along plane) and mg cosθ (perpendicular). Normal force N = mg cosθ. No friction yet.
simulation_states.emphasis: Weight has TWO components on an incline — resolve before applying Newton's laws

simulation_states.label: Frictionless incline
simulation_states.description: No friction: block accelerates at a = g sinθ. Independent of mass! Heavy and light blocks slide together. Steeper angle → faster acceleration.
simulation_states.emphasis: Without friction, acceleration depends only on angle — mass cancels out

simulation_states.label: With friction
simulation_states.description: Friction f = μN = μmg cosθ acts up the plane. Net force along plane: mg sinθ - μmg cosθ. At angle of repose (tanθ = μ): net force = 0, block just starts to slide.
simulation_states.emphasis: As θ increases, driving force grows AND friction capacity shrinks → double effect

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts incline angle and friction coefficient. Block slides or stays put. FBD updates. Green/red indicator for motion/rest. Angle of repose calculated.
simulation_states.emphasis: Increase angle until block starts sliding — that's the angle of repose!

common_misconceptions.belief: Normal force on an incline equals mg
common_misconceptions.correction: On an incline, N = mg cosθ, which is LESS than mg. The steeper the incline, the smaller the normal force. N = mg only on a horizontal surface (θ = 0).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show N bar shrinking as angle increases — N = mg cosθ clearly less than mg

common_misconceptions.belief: Heavier objects slide down faster on a frictionless incline
common_misconceptions.correction: On a frictionless incline, acceleration a = g sinθ is independent of mass — all objects accelerate at the same rate regardless of their weight. Mass cancels in F = ma: mg sinθ = ma → a = g sinθ.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Drop two different masses on frictionless incline — they slide together
